item.title: Coordinate systems
item.description: Note on curvilinear coordinates Suppose an arbitrary transformation from a cartesian \(\bm{r}=(x,y,z)\) to an arbitrary orthogonal curvilinear coordinate space \(\bm{\rho}=(\xi,\eta,\zeta)\) , where \(x = f_x(\xi,\eta,\zeta)\) , \(y = f_y(\xi,\eta,\zeta)\) and \(z = f_z(\xi,\eta,\zeta)\) . We define \[h_\xi = \left|\frac{\partial\bm{r}}{\partial\xi}\right|,~~~ h_\eta = \left|\frac{\partial\bm{r}}{\partial\eta}\right|,~~~ h_\zeta = \left|\frac{\partial\bm{r}}{\partial\zeta}\right|\] and the unit vectors in the new coordinate system: \[\hat{\bm{e}}_\xi=\frac{1}{h_\xi}\frac{\partial\bm{r}}{\partial\xi},~~~ \hat{\bm{e}}_\eta=\frac{1}{h_\eta}\frac{\partial\bm{r}}{\partial\eta},~~~ \hat{\bm{e}}_\zeta=\frac{1}{h_\zeta}\frac{\partial\bm{r}}{\partial\zeta}\] Velocities (or any other vector) can be converted (from curvilinear to Cartesian) via:
